verses.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे |
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः ||
verses.transliteration: sri bhagavan uvacha | ashochyan anvasochas tvam prajnavadans cha bhasase | gatasun agatasuns cha nanushochanti panditah
verses.hindi_meaning: श्रीभगवान बोले — तू उनके लिए शोक कर रहा है जो शोक के योग्य नहीं, और ज्ञान की बातें भी कह रहा है। ज्ञानी पुरुष न मृतक के लिए शोक करते हैं न जीवित के लिए।
verses.english_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: You grieve for those who should not be grieved for, yet speak words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for the living nor for the dead.
verses.context: Krishna's opening response to Arjuna's despair on the Kurukshetra battlefield, introducing the doctrine of the immortal Atman.
verses.practical_application: When grief overwhelms you, pause and ask: am I mourning the body or the soul? The Atman is never born, never dies — sorrow born of body-identification dissolves in this knowledge.

verses.sanskrit:
मात्रास्पर्शास्तु कौन्तेय शीतोष्णसुखदुःखदाः |
आगमापायिनोऽनित्यास्तांस्तितिक्षस्व भारत ||
verses.transliteration: matra-sparshas tu kaunteya shitoshna-sukha-duhkha-dah | agamapayino nityas tams titikshasva bharata
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कौन्तेय! सर्दी-गर्मी और सुख-दुःख देने वाले इन्द्रिय-विषयों के संयोग तो आते-जाते रहते हैं, वे अनित्य हैं। हे भारत! तू उन्हें सहन कर।
verses.english_meaning: O son of Kunti, the contacts of senses with their objects, giving rise to cold and heat, pleasure and pain, are transient and impermanent. Bear them, O Arjuna.
verses.context: Krishna teaches equanimity toward sensory experience — the philosophical basis for titiksha (forbearance) in brahmacharya.
verses.practical_application: Urges and cravings are like cold and heat — they arise and pass. Observe them without acting. The sadhaka who endures without reacting becomes established in inner steadiness.

verses.sanskrit:
अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् |
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति ||
verses.transliteration: avinashi tu tad viddhi yena sarvam idam tatam | vinasham avyayasyasya na kashchit kartum arhati
verses.hindi_meaning: किन्तु यह जान कि जिससे यह सारा संसार व्याप्त है, वह अविनाशी है। उस अव्यय का विनाश करने में कोई भी समर्थ नहीं है।
verses.english_meaning: Know that to be indestructible by which all this is pervaded. None is able to cause the destruction of that imperishable.
verses.context: The verse defines the Atman as all-pervading and indestructible — the bedrock of Vedantic teaching on the Self.
verses.practical_application: Your true Self cannot be diminished by failure, temptation, or past mistakes. Rooting your identity in the Atman rather than the body builds unshakeable inner strength.

verses.sanskrit:
न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्
नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः |
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ||
verses.transliteration: na jayate mriyate va kadachin nayam bhutva bhavita va na bhuyah | ajo nityah shashvato yam purano na hanyate hanyamane sharire
verses.hindi_meaning: यह आत्मा किसी काल में भी न जन्म लेता है न मरता है, और न यह उत्पन्न होकर फिर होने वाला है। यह अजन्मा, नित्य, शाश्वत, पुरातन है — शरीर के मारे जाने पर भी यह नहीं मारा जाता।
verses.english_meaning: The soul is never born nor dies at any time. It has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. It is unborn, eternal, ever-existing, and primeval. It is not slain when the body is slain.
verses.context: The most direct statement of Atman's immortality in the Gita — the foundation of fearlessness in spiritual practice.
verses.practical_application: Fear of loss, failure, or death loses its power when you truly know you are the deathless Atman. Brahmacharya becomes natural when identity shifts from the perishable body to the imperishable Self.

verses.sanskrit:
नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः |
न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः ||
verses.transliteration: nainam chhindanti shastrani nainam dahati pavakah | na chainam kledayanty apo na shoshayati marutah
verses.hindi_meaning: इस आत्मा को शस्त्र नहीं काट सकते, अग्नि नहीं जला सकती, जल नहीं भिगो सकता और वायु नहीं सुखा सकती।
verses.english_meaning: The soul can never be cut into pieces by any weapon, nor burned by fire, nor moistened by water, nor withered by the wind.
verses.context: Krishna uses four classical elements to emphasize the Atman's complete invulnerability to all material forces.
verses.practical_application: No external circumstance — failure, ridicule, temptation — can truly harm your inner Self. This verse is a source of extraordinary courage for the sadhaka facing difficult days.

verses.sanskrit:
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिरेकेह कुरुनन्दन |
बहुशाखा ह्यनन्ताश्च बुद्धयोऽव्यवसायिनाम् ||
verses.transliteration: vyavasayatmika buddhir ekeha kurunandana | bahu-shakha hy anantash cha buddhayo vyavasayinam
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कुरुनन्दन! इस विषय में निश्चयात्मिका बुद्धि एक ही होती है, परन्तु अस्थिर मनुष्यों की बुद्धियाँ अनेक शाखाओं वाली और अनन्त होती हैं।
verses.english_meaning: O son of Kuru, the resolute intelligence is single-pointed here; but the thoughts of the irresolute are many-branched and endless.
verses.context: Krishna contrasts vyavasayatmika buddhi (one-pointed resolve) with the scattered mind that jumps between countless desires.
verses.practical_application: A brahmacharya vow requires one-pointed resolve. When the mind branches into 'maybe tomorrow', 'just this once', it is the sign of avyavasayi buddhi. Sharpen your sankalpa to a single edge.

verses.sanskrit:
त्रैगुण्यविषया वेदा निस्त्रैगुण्यो भवार्जुन |
निर्द्वन्द्वो नित्यसत्त्वस्थो निर्योगक्षेम आत्मवान् ||
verses.transliteration: trai-gunya-vishaya veda nistrai-gunyo bhavarjuna | nirdvandvo nitya-sattva-stho niryoga-kshema atmavan
verses.hindi_meaning: हे अर्जुन! वेद तीनों गुणों के विषय को कहते हैं। तू तीनों गुणों से ऊपर उठ, द्वन्द्वों से रहित, नित्य सत्त्व में स्थित, योगक्षेम से रहित और आत्मपरायण हो।
verses.english_meaning: The Vedas deal with the three gunas; be free from the three gunas, O Arjuna. Be free from the pairs of opposites, ever established in sattva, free from acquisition and preservation, and be established in the Self.
verses.context: Krishna calls Arjuna to transcend the plane of the three gunas — rajas, tamas, sattva — and abide in pure Self-awareness.
verses.practical_application: Spiritual practice is not about accumulating merit (yoga-kshema) but about transcending all grasping. Rest in the Self without craving reward or fearing loss.

verses.sanskrit:
कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन |
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि ||
verses.transliteration: karmany evadhikaras te ma phaleshu kadachana | ma karma-phala-hetur bhur ma te sango stv akarmani
verses.hindi_meaning: तेरा कर्म करने में ही अधिकार है, फलों में कभी नहीं। तू कर्मफल का हेतु मत बन और तेरी आसक्ति अकर्म में भी न हो।
verses.english_meaning: Your right is to perform your duty only, and never to its fruits. Let not the fruits of action be your motive, nor let your attachment be to inaction.
verses.context: The most famous verse of the Gita — the foundational teaching of Nishkama Karma (desireless action).
verses.practical_application: Do your sadhana without obsessing over results. Log your practice, observe your breath, write your reflections — and release attachment to whether you feel enlightened today. The effort is yours; the fruit is not.

verses.sanskrit:
योगस्थः कुरु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा धनञ्जय |
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः समो भूत्वा समत्वं योग उच्यते ||
verses.transliteration: yoga-sthah kuru karmani sangam tyaktva dhananjaya | siddhy-asiddhyoh samo bhutva samatvam yoga uchyate
verses.hindi_meaning: हे धनञ्जय! तू आसक्ति छोड़कर, सिद्धि और असिद्धि में समान रहकर, योग में स्थित होकर कर्म कर। समत्व को ही योग कहते हैं।
verses.english_meaning: Perform actions being steadfast in Yoga, renouncing attachment, and be balanced in success and failure. This evenness of mind is called Yoga.
verses.context: Krishna defines Yoga as samatvam — equanimity — not as extraordinary achievement but as inner steadiness amid all outcomes.
verses.practical_application: Whether your meditation was deep or distracted today, maintain the same practice tomorrow. Samatvam means the quality of your effort does not hinge on yesterday's result.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
प्रजहाति यदा कामान्सर्वान्पार्थ मनोगतान् |
आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते ||
verses.transliteration: sri bhagavan uvacha | prajahati yada kaman sarvan partha mano-gatan | atmany evatmana tushtah sthita-prajnas tadochyate
verses.hindi_meaning: श्रीभगवान बोले — हे पार्थ! जब मनुष्य मन में स्थित सभी कामनाओं को त्याग देता है और आत्मा से ही आत्मा में तृप्त रहता है, तब वह स्थितप्रज्ञ कहलाता है।
verses.english_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: O Partha, when one completely casts off all desires of the mind and is satisfied in the Self by the Self alone, then one is said to be of steady wisdom.
verses.context: Begins the famous Sthitaprajna section (2.55–72) — a portrait of the liberated sage, the ideal the sadhaka aspires toward.
verses.practical_application: The standard for brahmacharya is not suppression of desire but its natural falling away when one is content in the Self. Daily meditation builds this inner fullness so external pleasures lose their compulsive pull.

verses.sanskrit:
दुःखेष्वनुद्विग्नमनाः सुखेषु विगतस्पृहः |
वीतरागभयक्रोधः स्थितधीर्मुनिरुच्यते ||
verses.transliteration: duhkheshv anudvigna-manah suksheshu vigata-sprihah | vita-raga-bhaya-krodhah sthita-dhir munir uchyate
verses.hindi_meaning: जो दुःखों में उद्विग्न नहीं होता, सुखों में स्पृहा नहीं करता, और राग, भय तथा क्रोध से मुक्त है — ऐसे मुनि को स्थितप्रज्ञ कहते हैं।
verses.english_meaning: One whose mind is not shaken by misery, who does not hanker after pleasures, and who is free from attachment, fear, and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.
verses.context: The second verse of the Sthitaprajna portrait — defining inner steadiness through freedom from the three afflictions: raga, bhaya, krodha.
verses.practical_application: In brahmacharya sadhana, the three enemies are attachment (raga to pleasure), fear (bhaya of failure/judgment), and anger (krodha when denied). Recognise whichever one drives a relapse.

verses.sanskrit:
यदा संहरते चायं कूर्मोऽङ्गानीव सर्वशः |
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ||
verses.transliteration: yada samharate chayam kurmo nganivaM sarvashah | indriyanindriyanarthebhyas tasya prajna pratishthita
verses.hindi_meaning: जब यह पुरुष कछुए की भाँति सब ओर से अपने अंगों को जैसे समेट लेता है, इन्द्रियों को इन्द्रिय-विषयों से — तब उसकी प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठित है।
verses.english_meaning: When, like a tortoise withdrawing its limbs from all sides, one withdraws the senses from their objects, then one's wisdom is firmly established.
verses.context: The famous tortoise analogy for pratyahara — withdrawal of the senses, the fifth limb of Patanjali's Ashtanga Yoga.
verses.practical_application: Pratyahara is the core skill of brahmacharya. When you notice the senses reaching toward stimulation — screen, sound, touch — practice the tortoise withdrawal. It is a learnable skill, not a permanent state.

verses.sanskrit:
यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः |
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनि हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः ||
verses.transliteration: yatato hy api kaunteya purushasya vipashchitah | indriyani pramathini haranti prasabham manah
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कौन्तेय! यत्न करने वाले विद्वान पुरुष के भी इन्द्रियाँ बलपूर्वक मन को हर लेती हैं।
verses.english_meaning: O son of Kunti, the turbulent senses forcibly carry away the mind even of a wise person who strives for control.
verses.context: An honest admission from Krishna that sense-control is difficult even for the learned — building humility rather than overconfidence in sadhana.
verses.practical_application: If a wise and striving person can be ambushed by the senses, you need systems, not just willpower. Structure your environment, your sleep, your screen time — don't rely on resolution alone.

verses.sanskrit:
ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते |
सङ्गात्संजायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते ||
verses.transliteration: dhyayato vishayan pumsah sangas teshupajayate | sangat sanjayate kamah kamat krodho bhijayate
verses.hindi_meaning: विषयों का चिन्तन करने वाले पुरुष की उन विषयों में आसक्ति हो जाती है, आसक्ति से काम उत्पन्न होता है, काम से क्रोध उत्पन्न होता है।
verses.english_meaning: When a person dwells on sense objects, attachment to them arises. From attachment comes desire; from desire, anger is born.
verses.context: The first half of the 'chain of bondage' — the psychological sequence from thought to desire to anger.
verses.practical_application: This is the exact sequence of a relapse: dwelling on a thought → attachment → craving → frustration/anger when denied. Break it at the very first link — do not dwell on the thought.

verses.sanskrit:
क्रोधाद्भवति सम्मोहः सम्मोहात्स्मृतिविभ्रमः |
स्मृतिभ्रंशाद्बुद्धिनाशो बुद्धिनाशात्प्रणश्यति ||
verses.transliteration: krodhad bhavati sammohah sammohat smriti-vibhramah | smriti-bhramsad buddhi-nasho buddhi-nashat pranashyati
verses.hindi_meaning: क्रोध से मोह उत्पन्न होता है, मोह से स्मृति-भ्रम होता है, स्मृति-भ्रम से बुद्धि का नाश होता है और बुद्धि के नष्ट होने से मनुष्य का पतन हो जाता है।
verses.english_meaning: From anger comes delusion; from delusion, loss of memory; from loss of memory, destruction of intelligence; from destruction of intelligence, one perishes.
verses.context: The complete chain of fall — anger → delusion → memory loss → intellect destroyed → total ruin. One of the most psychologically profound verses in the Gita.
verses.practical_application: Understand that an urge spike is not just a moment of weakness — it is the beginning of a chain that ends in smriti-bhramsha (forgetting your sankalpa). Catch it before step one.

verses.sanskrit:
रागद्वेषवियुक्तैस्तु विषयानिन्द्रियैश्चरन् |
आत्मवश्यैर्विधेयात्मा प्रसादमधिगच्छति ||
verses.transliteration: raga-dvesha-viyuktais tu vishayan indriyaish charan | atma-vashyair vidheyatma prasadam adhigachhati
verses.hindi_meaning: परन्तु राग और द्वेष से रहित, अपने वश में किये हुए इन्द्रियों से विषयों में विचरण करने वाला संयमी पुरुष प्रसाद को प्राप्त होता है।
verses.english_meaning: But the self-controlled person who moves among sense objects with senses free from attraction and repulsion, under the command of the Self — attains purity of mind.
verses.context: The positive counter-verse to 2.62–63: the disciplined sage can engage the world without being swept away.
verses.practical_application: The goal is not to destroy the senses but to command them. You can use technology, be around people, live in the world — and still maintain brahmacharya — when raga and dvesha no longer drive the senses.

verses.sanskrit:
नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना |
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम् ||
verses.transliteration: nasti buddhir ayuktasya na chayuktasya bhavana | na chabhavayatah shantir ashantasya kutah sukham
verses.hindi_meaning: असंयमी के लिए बुद्धि नहीं है, और न उसके लिए भावना (ध्यान की वृत्ति) है। भावना-रहित के लिए शान्ति नहीं है। अशान्त के लिए सुख कहाँ?
verses.english_meaning: There is no wisdom for the unsteady, nor meditation for the unsteady, and for one without meditation there is no peace; and for one without peace, how can there be happiness?
verses.context: A logical chain: lack of yoga → lack of meditation → lack of peace → lack of happiness. The inverse of the spiritual ascent.
verses.practical_application: The one who skips meditation citing 'busyness' starts the chain of misery. Happiness is not possible without peace; peace is not possible without meditation; meditation is not possible without discipline.

verses.sanskrit:
इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते |
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि ||
verses.transliteration: indriyanan hi charatam yan mano nuvidhiyate | tad asya harati prajnam vayur navam ivambhasi
verses.hindi_meaning: क्योंकि जैसे जल में चलती हुई नाव को वायु हर ले जाती है, वैसे ही विचरते हुए इन्द्रियों में से जिस एक पर भी मन जाता है, वह उसकी बुद्धि को हर लेता है।
verses.english_meaning: For when the mind follows the wandering senses, it carries away one's wisdom, just as wind carries away a boat on water.
verses.context: The boat analogy illustrates how even a single sense dragging the mind off course can destroy all accumulated wisdom.
verses.practical_application: One unguarded moment — an unopened page, an unexpected image — and the mind follows. Steady the boat with the anchor of daily practice before it is swept downstream.

verses.sanskrit:
आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत् |
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी ||
verses.transliteration: apuryamanam achala-pratistham samudram apah pravishanti yadvat | tadvat kama yam pravishanti sarve sa shantim apnoti na kama-kami
verses.hindi_meaning: जैसे चारों ओर से परिपूर्ण, अचल प्रतिष्ठा वाले समुद्र में नदियों का जल समाता जाता है वैसे ही जिस पुरुष में सब काम समा जाते हैं, वह शान्ति पाता है — न कि कामों को चाहने वाला।
verses.english_meaning: Just as the ocean remains unmoved though waters flow into it from all sides, so too the person into whom all desires flow without causing any disturbance attains peace — not the one who craves desires.
verses.context: The ocean analogy for the sthitaprajna — desires arise but cannot disturb the one who is vast and full inside.
verses.practical_application: The aspirant full of inner contentment (Atman-tushti) is like the ocean — desires enter but create no agitation. Build this inner fullness through meditation, svadhyaya, and surrendered action.

verses.sanskrit:
विहाय कामान्यः सर्वान्पुमांश्चरति निःस्पृहः |
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः स शान्तिमधिगच्छति ||
verses.transliteration: vihaya kaman yah sarvan pumans charati nihsprihah | nirmamo nirahankara sa shantim adhigachhati
verses.hindi_meaning: जो पुरुष सब कामनाओं को त्याग कर, स्पृहा रहित, ममता रहित और अहंकार रहित होकर विचरता है — वह शान्ति प्राप्त करता है।
verses.english_meaning: One who has abandoned all desires and moves about free from longing, without the sense of 'mine' and without ego — that person attains peace.
verses.context: The closing portrait of the liberated person: no longing (nihsprihah), no possessiveness (nirmama), no ego (nirahamkara).
verses.practical_application: Nirmama and nirahamkara are two roots of compulsive behaviour — possessiveness ('my pleasure') and ego ('I need this'). Observe which drives your urges and apply the antidote from this verse.

verses.sanskrit:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्नुते |
न च संन्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति ||
verses.transliteration: na karmananam arambhan naishkarmyam purusho shnute | na cha sannyasanad eva siddhim samadhigachhati
verses.hindi_meaning: मनुष्य कर्मों का आरम्भ न करने से नैष्कर्म्य को नहीं प्राप्त होता, और न केवल संन्यास से सिद्धि प्राप्त होती है।
verses.english_meaning: By not undertaking action, one does not attain freedom from action; nor does one attain perfection by mere renunciation.
verses.context: Krishna rejects both laziness and escapist renunciation — authentic karma yoga requires engaged action, not avoidance.
verses.practical_application: You cannot achieve brahmacharya by simply avoiding life. Engage your energy in constructive action — study, service, creative work. Idle time is the primary breeding ground for vikara.

verses.sanskrit:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत् |
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः ||
verses.transliteration: na hi kashchit kshanam api jatu tishthaty akarmakrit | karyate hy avashah karma sarvah prakriti-jair gunaih
verses.hindi_meaning: वास्तव में कोई भी क्षण भर भी बिना कर्म किये नहीं रह सकता। प्रकृति के गुणों से विवश होकर सबसे कर्म कराया ही जाता है।
verses.english_meaning: Indeed no one can remain even for a moment without performing action; for everyone is made to act helplessly by the qualities born of nature.
verses.context: The inevitability of action — action happens through the gunas of Prakriti whether you will it or not.
verses.practical_application: Since action is inevitable, the only choice is what kind. Tamas makes you act toward indulgence, rajas toward agitation — sattva guides action toward discipline. Cultivate sattva through diet, sleep, company.

verses.sanskrit:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः |
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर ||
verses.transliteration: yajnarthat karmano nyatra loko yam karma-bandhanah | tad-artham karma kaunteya mukta-sangah samachara
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कौन्तेय! यज्ञ के लिए किये गए कर्म के अतिरिक्त और सभी कर्म इस लोक में बन्धनकारक हैं। अतः तू आसक्ति-रहित होकर यज्ञ के निमित्त कर्म कर।
verses.english_meaning: This world is bound by action except when it is done as sacrifice. O son of Kunti, perform action as sacrifice, free from attachment.
verses.context: Every action done as an offering (yajna) is liberating; action done for personal gain becomes a bond.
verses.practical_application: Transform daily practice — journaling, exercise, learning — into yajna by offering the activity to a higher purpose. This removes the ego's grip and makes the practice sustainable.

verses.sanskrit:
एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं नानुवर्तयतीह यः |
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो मोघं पार्थ स जीवति ||
verses.transliteration: evam pravartitam chakram nanuvartayatiha yah | aghayur indriyaramo mogham partha sa jivati
verses.hindi_meaning: हे पार्थ! जो पुरुष इस लोक में इस प्रकार चलाये हुए चक्र का अनुसरण नहीं करता, वह इन्द्रियों में रमण करने वाला पापायु व्यर्थ जीता है।
verses.english_meaning: O Partha, one who does not follow in this world the wheel of creation thus set in motion, lives in vain, delighting in the senses, living a life of sin.
verses.context: The wheel of creation (chakra) refers to the dharmic cycle of contribution and consumption — the one who only consumes without contributing lives in vain.
verses.practical_application: A life spent in indulgence alone — consuming media, food, pleasure — without giving back through work, service, learning — is mogham (wasted). Brahmacharya redirects energy toward contribution.

verses.sanskrit:
तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर |
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः ||
verses.transliteration: tasmad asaktah satatam karyam karma samachara | asakto hy acharan karma param apnoti purushah
verses.hindi_meaning: इसलिए तू सदा आसक्ति रहित होकर कर्तव्य-कर्म का आचरण कर। आसक्ति रहित होकर कर्म करने से ही मनुष्य परम को प्राप्त होता है।
verses.english_meaning: Therefore, always perform your duty without attachment. By performing action without attachment, one attains the Supreme.
verses.context: The practical distillation of Karma Yoga — unattached action as the path to the Highest.
verses.practical_application: The word 'satatam' (always, continuously) is key. Spiritual practice is not an event; it is a continuous disposition. Bring non-attachment to every small action throughout the day.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः |
अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा कर्ताहमिति मन्यते ||
verses.transliteration: prakriteh kriyamanani gunaih karmani sarvashah | ahankara-vimudhatma kartaham iti manyate
verses.hindi_meaning: वास्तव में सभी कर्म प्रकृति के गुणों द्वारा किये जाते हैं, परन्तु अहंकार से मोहित आत्मा 'मैं कर्ता हूँ' ऐसा मान लेता है।
verses.english_meaning: All actions are performed in all cases by the gunas of nature; the person whose mind is deluded by ego thinks 'I am the doer.'
verses.context: The root of all bondage: the false identification of the Atman as the doer of actions driven by Prakriti's gunas.
verses.practical_application: When you take credit for a good streak or blame yourself for a relapse, notice: this is ahankara. The Atman is the witness. This understanding dissolves both pride and shame — and makes honest self-observation possible.

verses.sanskrit:
सदृशं चेष्टते स्वस्याः प्रकृतेर्ज्ञानवानपि |
प्रकृतिं यान्ति भूतानि निग्रहः किं करिष्यति ||
verses.transliteration: sadrisham ceshtate svasyah prakriter jnanavan api | prakritim yanti bhutani nigrahah kim karishyati
verses.hindi_meaning: ज्ञानी मनुष्य भी अपनी प्रकृति के अनुसार चेष्टा करता है। सभी प्राणी अपनी प्रकृति का अनुसरण करते हैं। फिर निग्रह क्या करेगा?
verses.english_meaning: Even a person of knowledge acts in accordance with their own nature. All beings follow their nature; what will restraint accomplish?
verses.context: A realistic acknowledgment that nature (samskara, habit) is powerful — restraint alone is insufficient without transforming the nature underneath.
verses.practical_application: Willpower fights your nature; sadhana transforms it. Instead of brute restraint, focus on building new samskaras through consistent positive action — that is what actually changes behaviour long-term.

verses.sanskrit:
इन्द्रियस्येन्द्रियस्यार्थे रागद्वेषौ व्यवस्थितौ |
तयोर्न वशमागच्छेत्तौ ह्यस्य परिपन्थिनौ ||
verses.transliteration: indriyasyendriyasyarthe raga-dveshau vyavasthitau | tayor na vasham agachchet tau hy asya paripanthinau
verses.hindi_meaning: प्रत्येक इन्द्रिय के विषय में राग और द्वेष दोनों स्थित हैं। मनुष्य को इन दोनों के वश में नहीं होना चाहिए — ये दोनों उसके शत्रु हैं।
verses.english_meaning: Attraction and repulsion are seated in every sense organ towards its objects. One should not come under the sway of these two; for they are obstructors of one's path.
verses.context: Raga (attraction) and dvesha (repulsion) are the fundamental pair of psychological forces that bind the soul to sense objects.
verses.practical_application: Both forms trap you: raga pulls you toward a craving; dvesha keeps you obsessed with the thing you're trying to avoid. Move through both as a witness, without either pursuing or resisting.

verses.sanskrit:
अथ केन प्रयुक्तोऽयं पापं चरति पूरुषः |
अनिच्छन्नपि वार्ष्णेय बलादिव नियोजितः ||
verses.transliteration: atha kena prayukto yam papam charati purushah | anichchhann api varshneya balad iva niyojitah
verses.hindi_meaning: हे वार्ष्णेय! फिर यह मनुष्य न चाहते हुए भी बलपूर्वक नियोजित-सा पाप का आचरण किस कारण करता है?
verses.english_meaning: O Varshneya, compelled by what does a person commit sinful acts, even unwillingly, as if driven by force?
verses.context: Arjuna's brilliant question — the exact question every person asks about their own compulsive behaviour. Krishna's answer is kama (desire/lust).
verses.practical_application: This is your question: why do I keep doing what I don't want to do? The Gita's answer is kama, born of rajoguna. The solution is in the next verses: identify it, understand it, then cut it with self-knowledge.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
काम एष क्रोध एष रजोगुणसमुद्भवः |
महाशनो महापाप्मा विद्ध्येनमिह वैरिणम् ||
verses.transliteration: sri bhagavan uvacha | kama esha krodha esha rajo-guna-samudbhavah | maha-shano maha-papma viddhy enam iha vairinam
verses.hindi_meaning: श्रीभगवान बोले — यह काम है, यही क्रोध है — यह रजोगुण से उत्पन्न होता है। यह महा भक्षक और महा पापी है। इसे ही यहाँ शत्रु जान।
verses.english_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: It is desire, it is anger, born of the guna of rajas; it is all-devouring and greatly sinful. Know this to be the enemy here.
verses.context: Krishna names kama (desire) as the direct answer to Arjuna's question — the root enemy on the inner battlefield.
verses.practical_application: The enemy is not external temptation but internal kama born of rajas. This clarifies the battlefield: the war is within. Know your enemy precisely — its birthplace (rajas), its nature (all-devouring), its identity (desire).

verses.sanskrit:
इन्द्रियाणि मनो बुद्धिरस्याधिष्ठानमुच्यते |
एतैर्विमोहयत्येष ज्ञानमावृत्य देहिनम् ||
verses.transliteration: indriyani mano buddhir asyadhishthanam uchyate | etair vimohayaty esha jnanam avritya dehinam
verses.hindi_meaning: इन्द्रियाँ, मन और बुद्धि — ये इस (काम) के निवास-स्थान कहे गए हैं। यह इनके द्वारा ज्ञान को ढककर जीव को मोहित करता है।
verses.english_meaning: The senses, the mind, and the intellect are said to be the dwelling places of desire. Through these, it veils wisdom and deludes the embodied soul.
verses.context: Desire operates on three levels: sensory (indriya), emotional (manas), and intellectual (buddhi) — it can rationalise itself at every level.
verses.practical_application: Notice that kama doesn't just operate through the senses — it can dress itself up as intellectual justification ('I deserve this', 'just this once'). Wisdom-veiling at the buddhi level is the most dangerous.

verses.sanskrit:
तस्मात्त्वमिन्द्रियाण्यादौ नियम्य भरतर्षभ |
पाप्मानं प्रजहि ह्येनं ज्ञानविज्ञाननाशनम् ||
verses.transliteration: tasmat tvam indriyany adau niyamya bharatarshabha | papmanam prajahi hy enam jnana-vijnana-nashanam
verses.hindi_meaning: इसलिए हे भरतश्रेष्ठ! पहले इन्द्रियों को वश में करके, ज्ञान और विज्ञान का नाश करने वाले इस पाप-स्वरूप काम को नष्ट कर।
verses.english_meaning: Therefore, O best of the Bharatas, controlling the senses at the outset, slay this sinful destroyer of knowledge and wisdom.
verses.context: The practical instruction: begin by disciplining the senses — that is the entry point into self-mastery.
verses.practical_application: Indriya-nigraha (sense control) is the first step, not the last. You cannot conquer kama by confronting it directly — block its supply lines first by controlling what you see, hear, and consume.

verses.sanskrit:
इन्द्रियाणि पराण्याहुरिन्द्रियेभ्यः परं मनः |
मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्यो बुद्धेः परतस्तु सः ||
verses.transliteration: indriyani parany ahur indriyebhyah param manah | manasas tu para buddhir yo buddheh paratas tu sah
verses.hindi_meaning: इन्द्रियों को स्थूल शरीर से श्रेष्ठ कहते हैं, मन इन्द्रियों से श्रेष्ठ है, बुद्धि मन से भी श्रेष्ठ है, और जो बुद्धि से भी परे है — वह (आत्मा) है।
verses.english_meaning: The senses are said to be superior to the gross body; the mind is superior to the senses; the intellect is superior to the mind; and the one beyond the intellect is the Atman.
verses.context: The hierarchy of instruments: body → senses → mind → intellect → Atman. Control flows from higher to lower.
verses.practical_application: When a sense organ is tempted, engage the mind to redirect it. When the mind is overcome, invoke the intellect (discriminative reasoning). When the intellect fails, rest in the Atman as witness. Practice ascending the ladder.

verses.sanskrit:
एवं बुद्धेः परं बुद्ध्वा संस्तभ्यात्मानमात्मना |
जहि शत्रुं महाबाहो कामरूपं दुरासदम् ||
verses.transliteration: evam buddheh param buddhva samstabhyatmanam atmana | jahi shatrum maha-baho kama-rupam durasadam
verses.hindi_meaning: इस प्रकार बुद्धि से परे आत्मा को जानकर, आत्मा के द्वारा अपने मन को वश में करके, हे महाबाहो! दुर्जय शत्रु काम को मार।
verses.english_meaning: Thus knowing the Self to be beyond the intellect, and steadying the mind by the Self, O mighty-armed Arjuna, slay the formidable enemy in the form of desire.
verses.context: The closing instruction of Chapter 3: root yourself in the Atman (beyond intellect) and from that position conquer desire.
verses.practical_application: This is the ultimate technique: establish yourself as the Atman-witness, and from that vantage point, the enemy (kama) has no ground to stand on. Meditation that goes beyond the intellect into pure awareness is this very practice.

verses.sanskrit:
यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत |
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम् ||
verses.transliteration: yada yada hi dharmasya glanir bhavati bharata | abhyutthanam adharmasya tadatmanam srijamy aham
verses.hindi_meaning: हे भारत! जब-जब धर्म की हानि और अधर्म की वृद्धि होती है, तब-तब मैं अपने-आपको प्रकट करता हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: O descendant of Bharata, whenever there is a decline of righteousness and rise of unrighteousness, then I manifest Myself.
verses.context: The divine promise of avatara — God descends to restore dharma when it declines.
verses.practical_application: When your inner dharma (your sadhana, your resolve) is in decline, understand that divine grace responds. Calling out, praying sincerely, and turning inward opens the door for the inner teacher to arise.

verses.sanskrit:
परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम् |
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे ||
verses.transliteration: paritranaya sadhunam vinashaya cha dushkritam | dharma-samsthapanarthaya sambhavami yuge yuge
verses.hindi_meaning: साधुओं के परित्राण के लिए, दुष्कर्मियों के विनाश के लिए और धर्म की स्थापना के लिए मैं युगे-युगे प्रकट होता हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: For the protection of the good, for the destruction of evildoers, and for the establishment of righteousness, I take birth from age to age.
verses.context: The cosmic purpose of divine incarnation — protection, purification, and dharma-restoration — operating both in the cosmos and within the individual.
verses.practical_application: Read this at the personal level: the inner divinity arises to protect the good impulses in you, dissolve the bad ones, and establish dharma in your daily life. Invite this process through surrender.

verses.sanskrit:
कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः |
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत् ||
verses.transliteration: karmany akarma yah pashed akarmani cha karma yah | sa buddhiman manushyeshu sa yuktah kritsnakarmakrit
verses.hindi_meaning: जो कर्म में अकर्म देखता है और अकर्म में कर्म देखता है — वह मनुष्यों में बुद्धिमान है, वह योगी है और वह सम्पूर्ण कर्मों का करने वाला है।
verses.english_meaning: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is wise among humans; that person is in Yoga and is a performer of all actions.
verses.context: The paradox of Karma Yoga: the one who acts without the ego of doership is truly inaction; the one who sits idle with an active, restless mind is truly acting.
verses.practical_application: The deepest brahmacharya is not sitting still — it is acting fully while the inner witness remains unmoved. 'Doing nothing' with a restless, craving mind is karmically more active than purposeful, surrendered work.

verses.sanskrit:
न हि ज्ञानेन सदृशं पवित्रमिह विद्यते |
तत्स्वयं योगसंसिद्धः कालेनात्मनि विन्दति ||
verses.transliteration: na hi jnanena sadrisham pavitram iha vidyate | tat svayam yoga-samsiddhah kalenatmani vindati
verses.hindi_meaning: इस संसार में ज्ञान के समान पवित्र करने वाला और कुछ नहीं है। उस ज्ञान को कालान्तर में योग में सिद्ध हुआ मनुष्य अपने आप आत्मा में पाता है।
verses.english_meaning: There is nothing in this world as purifying as knowledge. One who is perfected in Yoga finds it within themselves in time.
verses.context: Jnana (Self-knowledge) is the ultimate purifier — all other sadhana prepares the ground for this direct inner discovery.
verses.practical_application: Your sadhana is not the goal — it is preparation for jnana. Every day of discipline is making the vessel purer so that the light of Self-knowledge can be found 'kalen' (in time) through consistent yoga.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रद्धावान्लभते ज्ञानं तत्परः संयतेन्द्रियः |
ज्ञानं लब्ध्वा परां शान्तिमचिरेणाधिगच्छति ||
verses.transliteration: shraddha-van labhate jnanam tat-parah samyatendriyah | jnanam labdhva param shantim achirenadhigachhati
verses.hindi_meaning: श्रद्धावान, तत्पर और संयत-इन्द्रिय पुरुष ज्ञान प्राप्त करता है, और ज्ञान प्राप्त करके शीघ्र ही परम शान्ति को प्राप्त होता है।
verses.english_meaning: One who has faith, who is devoted to it, and who has controlled the senses, obtains knowledge. Having obtained knowledge, one quickly attains supreme peace.
verses.context: Three prerequisites for jnana: shraddha (faith), tat-paratva (single-pointed focus), samyata-indriya (sense control).
verses.practical_application: Sense control (samyata-indriya) is the third prerequisite for jnana — not a tangent but a direct requirement for the highest knowledge. Your brahmacharya practice is jnana-preparation.

verses.sanskrit:
अज्ञश्चाश्रद्दधानश्च संशयात्मा विनश्यति |
नायं लोकोऽस्ति न परो न सुखं संशयात्मनः ||
verses.transliteration: ajnash chaashraddhadhanash cha samshayatma vinashyati | nayam loko sti na paro na sukham samshayatmanah
verses.hindi_meaning: अज्ञानी, श्रद्धाहीन और संशयात्मा का नाश होता है। संशयात्मा के लिए न यह लोक है, न परलोक और न सुख।
verses.english_meaning: The ignorant, the faithless, and the doubting self perish. For the doubting self, there is neither this world nor the other, nor any happiness.
verses.context: Doubt (samshaya) — particularly self-doubt that oscillates between commitment and abandonment — is explicitly identified as a destroyer.
verses.practical_application: Commit fully to your 90-day sankalpa. The 'maybe I'll continue, maybe I won't' attitude is the samshayatma that destroys. Even imperfect action with faith outperforms perfect theory with doubt.

verses.sanskrit:
तस्मादज्ञानसम्भूतं हृत्स्थं ज्ञानासिनात्मनः |
छित्त्वैनं संशयं योगमातिष्ठोत्तिष्ठ भारत ||
verses.transliteration: tasmad ajnana-sambhutam hrit-stham jnanasina tmanah | chhittv ainam samshayam yogam atishthottishtha bharata
verses.hindi_meaning: इसलिए हृदय में अज्ञान से उत्पन्न अपने इस संशय को ज्ञान-रूपी तलवार से काटकर, योग में स्थित हो जा और उठ खड़ा हो, हे भारत!
verses.english_meaning: Therefore, cutting with the sword of knowledge this doubt born of ignorance that abides in your heart, take refuge in Yoga and arise, O Bharata!
verses.context: The stirring close of Chapter 4 — Krishna urges Arjuna (and every aspirant) to cut doubt with the sword of jnana and rise.
verses.practical_application: On the days you doubt your practice, your capacity, your path — this verse is the answer. Cut the doubt with the sword of discrimination: 'What am I, really? The doubter or the witness of doubt?' Then act.

verses.sanskrit:
ब्रह्मण्याधाय कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा करोति यः |
लिप्यते न स पापेन पद्मपत्रमिवाम्भसा ||
verses.transliteration: brahmany adhaya karmani sangam tyaktva karoti yah | lipyate na sa papena padma-patram ivambhasa
verses.hindi_meaning: जो पुरुष सब कर्मों को ब्रह्म में अर्पण करके और आसक्ति छोड़कर कर्म करता है, वह पाप से लिप्त नहीं होता — जैसे कमल-पत्र जल से।
verses.english_meaning: One who performs all actions, renouncing attachment and dedicating them to Brahman, is not touched by sin, just as a lotus leaf is untouched by water.
verses.context: The lotus leaf analogy — the most beautiful image in the Gita for karma-yoga: full engagement without contamination.
verses.practical_application: Live in the world fully — work, relate, serve — but like a lotus: in the water of worldly activity but never wetted by it. This is the practical ideal for a grihasthi-sadhaka doing brahmacharya.

verses.sanskrit:
कायेन मनसा बुद्ध्या केवलैरिन्द्रियैरपि |
योगिनः कर्म कुर्वन्ति सङ्गं त्यक्त्वात्मशुद्धये ||
verses.transliteration: kayena manasa buddhya kevalair indriyair api | yoginah karma kurvanti sangam tyaktvat-masuddhaye
verses.hindi_meaning: योगीजन आसक्ति त्यागकर, केवल शरीर, मन, बुद्धि और इन्द्रियों से आत्मशुद्धि के लिए कर्म करते हैं।
verses.english_meaning: Yogis perform actions with the body, mind, intellect, and even with the senses alone, giving up attachment, for the purpose of self-purification.
verses.context: All instruments of action — body through senses — are employed, but the motive is self-purification (atma-shuddhi), not pleasure.
verses.practical_application: Reframe every action in your sadhana: exercise is not for the body but for atma-shuddhi; restraint is not self-denial but purification of the instrument. This reframing makes practice feel meaningful rather than punishing.

verses.sanskrit:
सर्वकर्माणि मनसा संन्यस्यास्ते सुखं वशी |
नवद्वारे पुरे देही नैव कुर्वन्न कारयन् ||
verses.transliteration: sarva-karmani manasa sannyasyaste sukham vashi | nava-dvare pure dehi naiva kurvan na karayan
verses.hindi_meaning: मन से सभी कर्मों का संन्यास करके संयमी देही, न कुछ करते हुए और न करवाते हुए, नौ द्वारों वाले शरीर-नगर में सुखपूर्वक रहता है।
verses.english_meaning: The self-controlled embodied soul, having mentally renounced all actions, rests happily in the nine-gated city (the body), neither acting nor causing others to act.
verses.context: The body is described as a nine-gated city (two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, and two lower openings) — the soul dwells peacefully within when it renounces identification with actions.
verses.practical_application: The nine gates are entry points for sense experience. The wise one monitors them without obsession — watching what enters and exits without agitation. This is the inner posture of the brahmacharya practitioner.

verses.sanskrit:
विद्याविनयसम्पन्ने ब्राह्मणे गवि हस्तिनि |
शुनि चैव श्वपाके च पण्डिताः समदर्शिनः ||
verses.transliteration: vidya-vinaya-sampanne brahmane gavi hastini | shuni chaiva shvapake cha panditah sama-darsinah
verses.hindi_meaning: ज्ञानी लोग विद्या और विनय से युक्त ब्राह्मण में, गाय में, हाथी में, कुत्ते में और चाण्डाल में भी समान देखते हैं।
verses.english_meaning: The wise see the same in a learned and humble Brahmin, a cow, an elephant, a dog, and an outcaste.
verses.context: Sama-darshana (equal vision) is the hallmark of the jnani — seeing the one Self in all forms.
verses.practical_application: Sama-darshana dissolves othering and comparison, both of which feed ego. When you see the same Atman in all beings, competition, resentment, and lust — which all require otherness — begin to dissolve.

verses.sanskrit:
बाह्यस्पर्शेष्वसक्तात्मा विन्दत्यात्मनि यत्सुखम् |
स ब्रह्मयोगयुक्तात्मा सुखमक्षयमश्नुते ||
verses.transliteration: bahya-sparshe sv asaktama vindaty atmani yat sukham | sa brahma-yoga-yuktatma sukham akshayam ashnute
verses.hindi_meaning: जो पुरुष बाहरी स्पर्शों में आसक्त न होकर आत्मा में सुख पाता है, वह ब्रह्मयोग में युक्त आत्मा है और अक्षय सुख भोगता है।
verses.english_meaning: The person whose self is unattached to external contacts and finds happiness in the Atman, that self united with Brahma-yoga enjoys imperishable bliss.
verses.context: The direct alternative to sense-pleasure: joy found in the Atman is akshaya (indestructible), unlike all external pleasures.
verses.practical_application: Daily meditation practice is the direct route to this inner akshaya sukha. Each meditation session that goes inward builds familiarity with the source of joy that is not dependent on circumstances.

verses.sanskrit:
ये हि संस्पर्शजा भोगा दुःखयोनय एव ते |
आद्यन्तवन्तः कौन्तेय न तेषु रमते बुधः ||
verses.transliteration: ye hi samsparsha-ja bhoga duhkha-yonaya eva te | ady-antavantah kaunteya na teshu ramate budhah
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कौन्तेय! जो भोग संस्पर्श (इन्द्रिय-विषय-संयोग) से उत्पन्न होते हैं, वे सब दुःख के ही कारण हैं। वे आदि-अन्त वाले हैं — बुद्धिमान उनमें रमण नहीं करता।
verses.english_meaning: The enjoyments born of sense-contacts are sources of pain alone. They have a beginning and an end, O son of Kunti. The wise do not delight in them.
verses.context: Sense pleasures are duhkha-yonaya (wombs of sorrow) — they appear pleasurable but give birth to suffering because they are impermanent.
verses.practical_application: Every sensory indulgence has an arc: anticipation → momentary pleasure → withdrawal → craving again. Recognise this arc and you see the duhkha built into the very structure of sense-pleasure. The wise see the arc before starting it.

verses.sanskrit:
शक्नोतीहैव यः सोढुं प्राक्शरीरविमोक्षणात् |
कामक्रोधोद्भवं वेगं स युक्तः स सुखी नरः ||
verses.transliteration: shaknotih aiva yah sodhum prak sharira-vimokshanat | kama-krodhodbhavam vegam sa yuktah sa sukhi narah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो पुरुष शरीर छोड़ने से पहले ही इस लोक में काम और क्रोध से उत्पन्न वेग को सहन करने में समर्थ है — वही युक्त है और वही सुखी मनुष्य है।
verses.english_meaning: One who is able, here in this very life before death, to withstand the impulse born of desire and anger — that person is a yogi, and that person is happy.
verses.context: The highest definition of a yogi: the one who can tolerate the urge (vega) of desire and anger within this very lifetime.
verses.practical_application: This is precisely the brahmacharya challenge: can you tolerate (sodhum) the vega of kama and krodha without acting on it? Each time you do, you become more yukta. The 90-day sadhana is the training ground for this exact capacity.

verses.sanskrit:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत् |
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः ||
verses.transliteration: uddhared atmanatmanam natmanam avasadayet | atmaiva hy atmano bandhur atmaiva ripur atmanah
verses.hindi_meaning: मनुष्य को चाहिए कि अपने द्वारा अपना उद्धार करे, अपना पतन न करे। आत्मा ही अपना मित्र है और आत्मा ही अपना शत्रु है।
verses.english_meaning: Let a person raise himself by himself; let him not degrade himself. For the self alone is the friend of the self, and the self alone is the enemy of the self.
verses.context: The famous verse of self-responsibility — you are both your own liberator and your own destroyer. No external force determines your fate.
verses.practical_application: No one can save you from compulsive patterns except yourself. No teacher, app, or accountability partner can substitute for the inner decision. 'Uddhared atmanatmanam' — raise yourself, by yourself. Start today.

verses.sanskrit:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः |
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत् ||
verses.transliteration: bandhur atmatmanas tasya yenatmaivat mana jitah | anatmanas tu shatruttve varteta tmaiva shatru-vat
verses.hindi_meaning: जिसने अपने-आप से अपने आप को जीत लिया है, उसके लिए आत्मा ही मित्र है, परन्तु जिसने अपने आप को नहीं जीता — उसके लिए आत्मा ही शत्रु की भाँति रहता है।
verses.english_meaning: For one who has conquered the self by the self, the self is a friend; but for one who has not conquered the self, the self remains hostile like an enemy.
verses.context: The extension of 6.5 — the conquered self (jitena) becomes an ally; the unconquered self works against you from within.
verses.practical_application: An unconquered mind will rationalise, deceive, and negotiate away your resolve. A conquered mind becomes your greatest ally. The 90-day sadhana is precisely the process of converting enemy to friend.

verses.sanskrit:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः |
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः ||
verses.transliteration: yogi yunjita satatam atmanam rahasi sthitah | ekaki yata-chittatma nirashir aparigraha
verses.hindi_meaning: योगी को चाहिए कि वह एकांत में स्थित होकर, अकेला, मन और आत्मा को वश में किये, निराश (अपेक्षा-रहित) और परिग्रह-रहित होकर सदा अपने मन को ध्यान में लगाये।
verses.english_meaning: The yogi should constantly practice concentration of the mind, remaining in seclusion and alone, with mind and body controlled, free from desires and possessions.
verses.context: The practical instructions for dhyana yoga: solitude, aloneness, controlled mind, freedom from desire and accumulation.
verses.practical_application: Daily solitary meditation is non-negotiable. 'Rahasi sthitah' means a physical space that is yours alone — your meditation corner, your early morning time. Guard it as the core of your practice.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः |
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः ||
verses.transliteration: prashant-atma vigata-bhir brahmachari-vrate sthitah | manah samyamya mac-chitto yukta asita mat-parah
verses.hindi_meaning: शान्त अन्तःकरण, भयरहित, ब्रह्मचर्यव्रत में स्थित, मन को संयमित करके मुझमें चित्त लगाये हुए, युक्त होकर मुझे परायण होकर ध्यान करे।
verses.english_meaning: Serene-minded, fearless, firmly established in the vow of brahmacharya, with the mind controlled and fixed on Me — let the yogi sit in Yoga, dedicated to Me.
verses.context: The only verse in the Gita that directly names brahmacharya as a prerequisite for dhyana — establishing it as essential to the meditation practice.
verses.practical_application: Brahmacharya is not an optional supplement to meditation — Krishna places it as a direct prerequisite. 'Brahmachari-vrate sthitah' means established in the vow. Your brahmacharya sadhana is directly enabling your capacity to meditate.

verses.sanskrit:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः |
न चातिस्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन ||
verses.transliteration: naty-ashnatas tu yogo sti na chaikantam anashnato | na chati-svapna-shilasya jagranto naiva charjuna
verses.hindi_meaning: हे अर्जुन! बहुत खाने वाले का, बिल्कुल न खाने वाले का, बहुत सोने वाले का और सदा जागने वाले का योग नहीं होता।
verses.english_meaning: Yoga is not for one who eats too much or fasts completely, nor for one who sleeps too much or who stays awake excessively, O Arjuna.
verses.context: The Gita rejects all extremes in the practice of yoga — both excess and total deprivation are obstacles.
verses.practical_application: Extreme fasting, sleep deprivation, or rigid over-restriction are not brahmacharya — they are rajasic extremes that destabilise the nervous system and increase vulnerability to urges. The Gita prescribes yukta-ahara (moderate diet).

verses.sanskrit:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु |
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा ||
verses.transliteration: yuktahara-viharasya yukta-ceshtasya karmasu | yukta-svapnavabodhasya yogo bhavati duhkha-ha
verses.hindi_meaning: यथायोग्य आहार-विहार करने वाले, कर्मों में यथायोग्य चेष्टा करने वाले और यथायोग्य सोने और जागने वाले — उसके लिए योग दुःखों का नाशक होता है।
verses.english_meaning: For one who is moderate in eating, recreation, exertion in work, and in sleep and waking — Yoga destroys all sorrow.
verses.context: The positive prescription: yukta (moderate, balanced) in food, movement, work, sleep — this makes yoga effective and sustainable.
verses.practical_application: Build your daily schedule around yukta-ahara (sattvic food), yukta-vihara (healthy exercise), yukta-karma (purposeful work), yukta-svapna (7-8 hours of quality sleep). This infrastructure is what makes brahmacharya possible.

verses.sanskrit:
यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता |
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः ||
verses.transliteration: yatha dipo nivata-stho nengite sopama smrita | yogino yata-chittasya yunjato yogam atmanah
verses.hindi_meaning: जैसे वायुरहित स्थान में दीपक की लौ नहीं हिलती — यही उपमा योगमग्न और संयमित चित्त वाले योगी के विषय में कही गयी है।
verses.english_meaning: As a lamp in a windless place does not flicker — this is the simile used for the yogi of controlled mind practising yoga of the Self.
verses.context: The lamp-in-windless-place analogy for the steady meditating mind — the classic image of dhyana.
verses.practical_application: Your meditation practice is the training of this steadiness. At first the flame flickers. With practice it becomes still. Each sit, whether turbulent or calm, contributes to reducing the internal 'wind' of restless thought.

verses.sanskrit:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया |
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति ||
verses.transliteration: yatroparamate chittam niruddham yoga-sevaya | yatra chaivatmanatmanam pashyann atmani tushyati
verses.hindi_meaning: जहाँ योगाभ्यास से निरुद्ध चित्त उपराम होता है और जहाँ आत्मा के द्वारा आत्मा को देखकर आत्मा में ही सन्तुष्ट रहता है।
verses.english_meaning: Where the mind, restrained by the practice of Yoga, becomes still, and where seeing the Self by the Self, one is satisfied within the Self.
verses.context: The description of samadhi — the mind becomes still, the Self sees the Self, and perfect satisfaction arises from within.
verses.practical_application: This inner self-sufficiency is the goal: satisfaction that does not need external objects. As meditation deepens, the craving for external stimulation naturally diminishes because the inner source of joy is discovered.

verses.sanskrit:
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम् |
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः ||
verses.transliteration: sukham atyantikam yat tad buddhi-grahyam atindriyam | vetti yatra na chaivaayam sthitash chalati tattvata
verses.hindi_meaning: जहाँ अतीन्द्रिय और बुद्धि से जानने योग्य उस अत्यन्त सुख का अनुभव होता है, और जहाँ स्थित होने पर यह (योगी) तत्त्व से विचलित नहीं होता।
verses.english_meaning: Where one experiences that absolute happiness grasped by the intellect but beyond the senses, and where established, one does not deviate from the truth.
verses.context: The happiness of samadhi is 'atindriya' — transcending the senses entirely, available only to the purified buddhi.
verses.practical_application: The highest yoga offers a joy that is beyond all sensory pleasure. This is not suppression — it is transcendence. When even a taste of this is known in meditation, the pull of sensory indulgence weakens permanently.

verses.sanskrit:
तं विद्याद्दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसञ्ज्ञितम् |
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा ||
verses.transliteration: tam vidyad duhkha-samyoga-viyogam yoga-sanjnitam | sa nishchayena yoktavyo yogo nirviNNa-chetasa
verses.hindi_meaning: दुःखों के संयोग से वियोग (अलगाव) को ही योग जानो। उस योग को निश्चय और उत्साहित चित्त से करना चाहिए।
verses.english_meaning: Let it be known that the severance from union with sorrow is called Yoga. This Yoga must be practised with determination and with an undepressed mind.
verses.context: Yoga is defined as duhkha-samyoga-viyoga — the disconnection from habitual bonding with suffering. Practised with resolve and non-despondency.
verses.practical_application: Depression about progress ('I keep failing, what's the point') is explicitly identified here as an obstacle. Yoga must be practiced with anirvinna-chetasa — an undepressed mind. Get up after a fall without despondency.

verses.sanskrit:
शनैः शनैरुपरमेद्बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया |
आत्मसंस्थं मनः कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत् ||
verses.transliteration: shanaih shanair uparamed buddhya dhritri-grhitaya | atma-samstham manah kritva na kincid api chintayet
verses.hindi_meaning: धैर्य से पकड़ी हुई बुद्धि के द्वारा धीरे-धीरे शान्त होते हुए मन को आत्मा में स्थित करके कुछ भी चिन्तन न करे।
verses.english_meaning: Gradually, step by step, with intellect held firm by conviction, let one withdraw the mind to rest in the Self; let one not think of anything at all.
verses.context: The practical instruction for meditation: gradual, patient withdrawal toward the Self, not forceful suppression.
verses.practical_application: Meditation is 'shanaih shanaih' — gradually, gradually. Do not force the mind. Gently guide it, patiently return it to the Self each time it wanders. Force creates resistance; patient firmness (dhritri) creates absorption.

verses.sanskrit:
यतो यतो निश्चरति मनश्चञ्चलमस्थिरम् |
ततस्ततो नियम्यैतदात्मन्येव वशं नयेत् ||
verses.transliteration: yato yato nishcharati manash chanchalam asthiram | tatas tato niyamyaitad atmany eva vasham nayet
verses.hindi_meaning: यह चंचल और अस्थिर मन जहाँ-जहाँ भी जाये, वहाँ-वहाँ से इसे हटाकर आत्मा में ही लगाये।
verses.english_meaning: Wherever the restless, unsteady mind wanders, from there, bringing it back each time, one should lead it under the control of the Atman alone.
verses.context: The simple, direct instruction for meditation when the mind wanders — bring it back, again and again, without frustration.
verses.practical_application: This is the complete technique: mind wanders → bring back → mind wanders → bring back. The 'bringing back' is the practice. There is no failure in wandering — only failure in not bringing back. Do this every day.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम् |
उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम् ||
verses.transliteration: prashanta-manasam hy enam yoginam sukham uttamam | upaiti shanta-rajasam brahma-bhutam akalmashsam
verses.hindi_meaning: क्योंकि शान्त मन वाले, शान्त रज वाले, पाप-रहित और ब्रह्म-रूप बने इस योगी को उत्तम सुख प्राप्त होता है।
verses.english_meaning: Supreme happiness indeed comes to the yogi whose mind is peaceful, whose rajas is calmed, who is free from sin, who has become one with Brahman.
verses.context: The fruit of perfected meditation: brahma-bhuta (becoming Brahman-natured), rajas calmed, impurities dissolved — supreme happiness.
verses.practical_application: Shanta-rajas (calmed rajas) is the key transformation in brahmacharya. Rajas is the guna that drives restless craving. As meditation quiets rajas, compulsive urges lose their energy. This is the mechanism of transformation.

verses.sanskrit:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद्दृढम् |
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम् ||
verses.transliteration: chanchalam hi manah krishna pramathi balavad dridham | tasyaham nigraham manye vayor iva sudushkaram
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कृष्ण! मन बड़ा चंचल, प्रमथनशील, बलवान और दृढ़ है। मैं इसके निग्रह को वायु रोकने के समान अत्यन्त दुष्कर मानता हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: The mind is indeed restless, turbulent, powerful, and obstinate, O Krishna. I think it is as difficult to control as the wind.
verses.context: Arjuna's honest confession — and yours. The mind is chanchala (restless), pramathi (turbulent), balavat (powerful), dridha (stubborn).
verses.practical_application: This acknowledgement is not defeat — it is clarity. You are not weak for finding the mind difficult; Arjuna himself says so. The important thing is that Krishna's answer (6.35) provides the actual method: abhyasa and vairagya.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम् |
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते ||
verses.transliteration: sri bhagavan uvacha | asamsayam maha-baho mano durnigraham chalam | abhyasena tu kaunteya vairagyena cha grhyate
verses.hindi_meaning: श्रीभगवान बोले — हे महाबाहो! निस्संदेह मन चंचल और कठिनता से वश में होने वाला है। परन्तु हे कौन्तेय! अभ्यास और वैराग्य से यह वश में किया जाता है।
verses.english_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: Undoubtedly, O mighty-armed, the mind is difficult to control and restless. But by practice and dispassion, O son of Kunti, it is controlled.
verses.context: The definitive answer: the mind is controlled by abhyasa (consistent practice) and vairagya (dispassion toward sense objects). No other method is given.
verses.practical_application: Two tools only: abhyasa (show up to practice daily, no exceptions) and vairagya (intellectual recognition that sense pleasures are duhkha-yonaya). Build both systematically. One without the other is incomplete.

verses.sanskrit:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः |
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः ||
verses.transliteration: asamyatatmana yogo dushprapa iti me matih | vashyatmana tu yatata shakyo vaptum upayatah
verses.hindi_meaning: असंयमी के लिए योग प्राप्त करना कठिन है — यह मेरा मत है। परन्तु वश में किये हुए मन वाले प्रयत्नशील के लिए उपाय से वह प्राप्त करना सम्भव है।
verses.english_meaning: Yoga is difficult to attain for one who is not self-controlled — this is My opinion. But for the self-controlled person who strives, it is attainable through proper means.
verses.context: Krishna closes the mind-control discussion: self-control is the prerequisite; striving with right means is the path.
verses.practical_application: Three elements: vashyatma (controlled self), yatata (striving), upayatah (through proper means). SadhanaOS is built on all three — the daily structure is the upaya. Consistent engagement with it builds vashyatma.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
मय्यासक्तमनाः पार्थ योगं युञ्जन्मदाश्रयः |
असंशयं समग्रं मां यथा ज्ञास्यसि तच्छृणु ||
verses.transliteration: sri bhagavan uvacha | mayy asakta-manah partha yogam yunjaj mad-ashrayah | asamshayam samagram mam yatha jnasyasi tach chhrnau
verses.hindi_meaning: श्रीभगवान बोले — हे पार्थ! मुझमें आसक्त मन से और मेरा आश्रय लेकर योग का अभ्यास करते हुए, तू जिस प्रकार मुझे सम्पूर्ण रूप से और निःसंशय जानेगा — वह सुन।
verses.english_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: O Partha, with your mind attached to Me, practising Yoga and taking refuge in Me, hear how you shall know Me in full, without any doubt.
verses.context: The opening of Chapter 7 (Jnana Vijnana Yoga) — knowledge of God in full through devoted practice.
verses.practical_application: 'Mad-ashrayah' (taking refuge in Me) means surrendering the doership of practice to the divine. When the practice feels like yours alone, ego makes it heavy. Surrender it: 'I practice in You, for You, as You.'

verses.sanskrit:
दैवी ह्येषा गुणमयी मम माया दुरत्यया |
मामेव ये प्रपद्यन्ते मायामेतां तरन्ति ते ||
verses.transliteration: daivi hy esha guna-mayi mama maya duratyaya | mam eva ye prapadyante mayam etam taranti te
verses.hindi_meaning: यह मेरी दैवी गुणमयी माया अत्यन्त दुस्तर है। जो केवल मुझे ही भजते हैं, वे इस माया को पार कर जाते हैं।
verses.english_meaning: This divine illusion of Mine, made of the three gunas, is hard to overcome. But those who take refuge in Me alone cross over this illusion.
verses.context: The power of maya (divine illusion formed of the three gunas) is immense — the only escape is surrender to the divine.
verses.practical_application: Rajas (restlessness) and tamas (inertia) are constituents of maya that drive compulsive behaviour. They are duratyaya (very difficult to cross) by effort alone. Surrender to the divine transforms them where willpower cannot.

verses.sanskrit:
बहूनां जन्मनामन्ते ज्ञानवान्मां प्रपद्यते |
वासुदेवः सर्वमिति स महात्मा सुदुर्लभः ||
verses.transliteration: bahunam janmanam ante jnanavan mam prapadyate | vasudevah sarvam iti sa mahatma su-durlabhah
verses.hindi_meaning: अनेक जन्मों के अन्त में ज्ञानी पुरुष 'वासुदेव ही सब कुछ है' — ऐसा जानकर मुझे भजता है। ऐसा महात्मा अत्यन्त दुर्लभ है।
verses.english_meaning: At the end of many births, the person of wisdom takes refuge in Me, knowing 'Vasudeva is all.' Such a great soul is very rare.
verses.context: The culmination of jnana: seeing Vasudeva (the divine) in everything — the ultimate non-dual realisation.
verses.practical_application: Each moment of sadhana is progress toward this. You may not attain 'Vasudeva is all' this lifetime, but each day of discipline moves you toward the rare mahAtma who sees this directly. The journey is the attainment.

verses.sanskrit:
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु मामनुस्मर युध्य च |
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्मामेवैष्यस्यसंशयम् ||
verses.transliteration: tasmat sarveshu kaleshu mam anusmara yudhya cha | mayy arpita-mano-buddhir mam evaishy asamsayam
verses.hindi_meaning: इसलिए सब कालों में मेरा स्मरण कर और युद्ध भी कर। मुझमें अर्पित मन और बुद्धि वाला तू नि:संदेह मुझे ही प्राप्त होगा।
verses.english_meaning: Therefore, at all times remember Me and fight. With your mind and intellect fixed on Me, you will certainly come to Me alone, without doubt.
verses.context: The instruction for constant remembrance — not periodic prayer but uninterrupted divine memory woven into all activity.
verses.practical_application: Make divine remembrance a habit woven through the day: a mantra during transitions, a moment of surrender before meals, awareness of the Atman during breath. This constant current of remembrance is the highest practice.

verses.sanskrit:
अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः |
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः ||
verses.transliteration: ananya-chetah satatam yo mam smarati nityashah | tasyaham sulabhah partha nitya-yuktasya yoginah
verses.hindi_meaning: हे पार्थ! जो अनन्य चित्त से सदा-नित्य मेरा स्मरण करता है — ऐसे नित्ययुक्त योगी के लिए मैं सुलभ हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: For those who remember Me with undivided attention, always and constantly, for those ever-steadfast yogis, I am easy to attain, O Partha.
verses.context: The divine promise: unbroken, undivided remembrance makes God easily accessible — sulabha (easy to obtain).
verses.practical_application: The word 'sulabhah' is striking — God becomes easy to attain through constant remembrance. The difficulty of spiritual life decreases proportionally to the consistency of divine remembrance. Build that consistency.

verses.sanskrit:
अनन्याश्चिन्तयन्तो मां ये जनाः पर्युपासते |
तेषां नित्याभियुक्तानां योगक्षेमं वहाम्यहम् ||
verses.transliteration: ananyash chintayanto mam ye janah paryupasate | tesham nityabhiyuktanam yoga-kshemam vahamy aham
verses.hindi_meaning: जो अनन्यभाव से मेरा चिन्तन करते हुए मेरी उपासना करते हैं, उन नित्ययुक्त भक्तों का योगक्षेम मैं वहन करता हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: For those who worship Me with devotion, meditating on My transcendental form, who are constantly devoted — I carry what they lack and preserve what they have.
verses.context: The famous divine promise of yoga-kshema: God personally takes care of both the acquisition (yoga) and preservation (kshema) of the devotee's welfare.
verses.practical_application: The devoted sadhaka does not need to anxiously manage every outcome. God promises to handle both providing and protecting. Surrender anxiety about the future of your practice — show up in devotion; let the divine handle the results.

verses.sanskrit:
पत्रं पुष्पं फलं तोयं यो मे भक्त्या प्रयच्छति |
तदहं भक्त्युपहृतमश्नामि प्रयतात्मनः ||
verses.transliteration: patram pushpam phalam toyam yo me bhaktya prayachhati | tad aham bhakty-upahrtam ashnami prayatatmanah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो भक्त मुझे श्रद्धा से पत्र, पुष्प, फल और जल अर्पण करता है, उस शुद्धात्मा की भक्तिपूर्वक अर्पित वस्तु को मैं स्वीकार करता हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: Whoever offers Me with devotion a leaf, a flower, a fruit, or water — that offering of love from the pure-hearted one I accept.
verses.context: The most accessible verse on devotional offering — God accepts simplicity given with love over grandeur given without.
verses.practical_application: Your daily practice — a moment of prayer, a breath of surrender, a verse read with attention — is the leaf, flower, fruit, and water. God accepts the simplest sincere offering. Begin where you are.

verses.sanskrit:
यत्करोषि यदश्नासि यज्जुहोषि ददासि यत् |
यत्तपस्यसि कौन्तेय तत्कुरुष्व मदर्पणम् ||
verses.transliteration: yat karoshi yad ashnasi yaj juhoshi dadasi yat | yat tapasyasi kaunteya tat kurushhva mad-arpanam
verses.hindi_meaning: हे कौन्तेय! तू जो कुछ करता है, जो खाता है, जो हवन करता है, जो दान करता है और जो तप करता है — वह सब मुझे अर्पण कर।
verses.english_meaning: O son of Kunti, whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer or give, whatever austerities you perform — do that as an offering to Me.
verses.context: The complete integration of life with spiritual practice — every single action becomes devotion through the intention of offering.
verses.practical_application: This is total yoga: eating → offered to God; working → offered to God; exercising → offered to God; practising brahmacharya → offered to God. When every act is offered, there is no secular realm left for compulsion to hide in.

verses.sanskrit:
मन्मना भव मद्भक्तो मद्याजी मां नमस्कुरु |
मामेवैष्यसि युक्त्वैवमात्मानं मत्परायणः ||
verses.transliteration: man-mana bhava mad-bhakto mad-yaji mam namaskuru | mam evaishyasi yuktvaivam atmanam mat-parayanah
verses.hindi_meaning: मुझमें मन लगा, मेरा भक्त बन, मेरा पूजन कर, मुझे नमस्कार कर — इस प्रकार मुझमें आत्मा को जोड़कर, मत्परायण होकर तू मुझे ही प्राप्त होगा।
verses.english_meaning: Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, worship Me, bow down to Me. So joining yourself to Me, with Me as your supreme goal, you shall come to Me.
verses.context: The quintessential bhakti instruction — four simple acts: think of God, be devoted, worship, bow. The path is simpler than the mind believes.
verses.practical_application: On difficult days of practice, return to this simplest of instructions: just think of God. Not philosophy, not technique — just turn the mind toward the divine for one moment. This is the practice when everything else seems too much.

verses.sanskrit:
अहमात्मा गुडाकेश सर्वभूताशयस्थितः |
अहमादिश्च मध्यं च भूतानामन्त एव च ||
verses.transliteration: aham atma gudakesha sarva-bhutashaya-sthitah | aham adish cha madhyam cha bhutanam anta eva cha
verses.hindi_meaning: हे गुडाकेश! मैं सभी प्राणियों के हृदय में स्थित आत्मा हूँ। मैं ही प्राणियों का आदि, मध्य और अन्त हूँ।
verses.english_meaning: I am the Self, O Gudakesha, seated in the hearts of all beings. I am the beginning, the middle, and the end of all beings.
verses.context: God's direct declaration of being the Atman within all — the ultimate non-dual statement of Chapter 10.
verses.practical_application: The divine dwells within you — not outside, not only in temples. Your sadhana is a turning inward to the God already present at the heart. This makes every meditation a direct encounter, not a distant prayer.

verses.sanskrit:
यद्यद्विभूतिमत्सत्त्वं श्रीमदूर्जितमेव वा |
तत्तदेवावगच्छ त्वं मम तेजोंऽशसम्भवम् ||
verses.transliteration: yad yad vibhutimat sattvam srimad urjitam eva va | tat tad evavagaccha tvam mama tejomsha-sambhavam
verses.hindi_meaning: जो-जो विभूतिशाली, श्रीसम्पन्न या शक्तिशाली वस्तु है — वह सब मेरे तेज के अंश से उत्पन्न समझ।
verses.english_meaning: Whatever is glorious, prosperous, or powerful — know that each such thing has arisen from just a spark of My splendour.
verses.context: All beauty, power, and excellence in creation are portions of divine glory — the world becomes a window into the divine through this vision.
verses.practical_application: When you encounter excellence — a beautiful sunrise, extraordinary music, a great mind — see it as a tejas-amsha (spark of divine splendour). This vision dissolves craving for possession and replaces it with reverent appreciation.

verses.sanskrit:
मत्कर्मकृन्मत्परमो मद्भक्तः सङ्गवर्जितः |
निर्वैरः सर्वभूतेषु यः स मामेति पाण्डव ||
verses.transliteration: mat-karma-krin mat-paramo mad-bhaktah sanga-varjitah | nirvaira sarvabhuteshu yah sa mam eti pandava
verses.hindi_meaning: जो मेरे लिए कर्म करता है, मुझे परम मानता है, मेरा भक्त है, आसक्ति-रहित है और सभी प्राणियों में वैर-रहित है — वह मुझे प्राप्त होता है, हे पाण्डव!
verses.english_meaning: One who does My work, who regards Me as the Supreme, who is devoted to Me, free from attachment, and without enmity toward all beings — that person comes to Me, O Pandava.
verses.context: The close of the Vishvarupa chapter — five qualities that bring one to God: service, supreme devotion, attachment-free action, and universal non-enmity.
verses.practical_application: Nirvaira sarvabhuteshu (no enmity toward any being) is a brahmacharya quality — the one free from lust and anger has no basis for enmity. This is the fruit: universal goodwill as a natural expression of the purified heart.

verses.sanskrit:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च |
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी ||
verses.transliteration: adveshta sarva-bhutanam maitrah karuna eva cha | nirmamo nirahankara sama-duhkha-sukhah kshami
verses.hindi_meaning: जो सब प्राणियों से द्वेष नहीं करता, मित्रभाव और करुणा वाला है, ममता और अहंकार से रहित है, दुःख-सुख में समान है और क्षमाशील है।
verses.english_meaning: One who has no ill will toward any being, who is friendly and compassionate, free from possessiveness and ego, equal in pain and pleasure, and forgiving.
verses.context: The first of the 'dear to Me' (priya) verses in Chapter 12 — a detailed portrait of the beloved devotee.
verses.practical_application: This is the character the sadhana is building. Brahmacharya removes the possessiveness (nirmama) and ego (nirahamkara) that distort relationships. The purified sadhaka becomes naturally maitri (friendly) and karuna (compassionate).

verses.sanskrit:
सन्तुष्टः सततं योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः |
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः ||
verses.transliteration: santushtha satatam yogi yatatma dridha-nishchayah | mayy arpita-mano-buddhir yo mad-bhaktah sa me priyah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो सदा सन्तुष्ट, योगी, संयमी और दृढ़ निश्चयी है, जिसने मन और बुद्धि मुझ पर अर्पण कर दिये हैं — ऐसा भक्त मुझे प्रिय है।
verses.english_meaning: One who is ever content, steady in yoga, self-controlled, of firm resolve, with mind and intellect offered to Me — such a devotee is dear to Me.
verses.context: The second 'priya' verse — contentment, steadiness, self-control, and firmness as the qualities God loves.
verses.practical_application: Santushtha satatam (ever content) is key — the practice is not about achieving a future state but being content now in the process. Dridha-nishchaya (firm resolve) is your sankalpa. These two together make practice sustainable.

verses.sanskrit:
यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको लोकान्नोद्विजते च यः |
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तो यः स च मे प्रियः ||
verses.transliteration: yasman nodvijate loko lokan nodvijate cha yah | harshamersha-bhayodvegair mukto yah sa cha me priyah
verses.hindi_meaning: जिससे लोग उद्विग्न नहीं होते और जो स्वयं लोगों से उद्विग्न नहीं होता, जो हर्ष, ईर्ष्या, भय और उद्वेग से मुक्त है — वह मुझे प्रिय है।
verses.english_meaning: One from whom the world is not disturbed, and who is not disturbed by the world, who is free from the emotions of joy, envy, fear, and anxiety — that person is dear to Me.
verses.context: Freedom from emotional reactivity in all directions — neither disturbing others nor being disturbed. The ideal of the established sadhaka.
verses.practical_application: Brahmacharya sadhana removes the reactivity that comes from unfulfilled craving. The one who does not need the world's validation or stimulation neither disturbs others nor is disturbed. This is the fruit of inner completeness.

verses.sanskrit:
अनपेक्षः शुचिर्दक्ष उदासीनो गतव्यथः |
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः ||
verses.transliteration: anapekshah shuchir daksha udasino gata-vyathah | sarvarambha-parityagi yo mad-bhaktah sa me priyah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो निःस्पृह, शुद्ध, दक्ष, उदासीन, व्यथा-रहित और सब प्रारम्भों (कर्म-फलों की आसक्ति) का त्यागी है — ऐसा भक्त मुझे प्रिय है।
verses.english_meaning: One who is free from dependence, pure, capable, detached, undistressed, who has relinquished all undertakings — such a devotee is dear to Me.
verses.context: The sixth 'priya' quality-cluster: freedom from neediness, outer and inner purity, skill, non-attachment, and fearlessness.
verses.practical_application: Anapeksha (free from dependence/expectation) is the opposite of the needy ego that craves validation. As brahmacharya builds inner strength, this independence naturally arises — you need less from the world.

verses.sanskrit:
यो न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति |
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्यः स मे प्रियः ||
verses.transliteration: yo na hrishyati na dveshti na shochati na kankshhati | shubhashubha-parityagi bhaktiman yah sa me priyah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो न हर्षित होता है, न द्वेष करता है, न शोक करता है, न आकांक्षा करता है, जो शुभ और अशुभ दोनों का त्यागी है — ऐसा भक्त मुझे प्रिय है।
verses.english_meaning: One who neither rejoices nor hates, neither grieves nor desires, who has given up both good and evil — such a devoted person is dear to Me.
verses.context: The stoic heart: untouched by fortune or misfortune, gain or loss — the complete inner freedom of the mature devotee.
verses.practical_application: On a 'good streak' day, do not over-rejoice (hrishyati) — that attachment creates the crash. On a fallen day, do not hate or grieve excessively. The same inner steadiness applies to both. Samatvam as a lived practice.

verses.sanskrit:
समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः |
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः सङ्गविवर्जितः ||
verses.transliteration: samah shatrau cha mitre cha tatha manapamanayoh | shitoshna-sukha-duhkheshu samah sanga-vivarjitah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो शत्रु और मित्र में, मान और अपमान में, शीत-ऊष्ण और सुख-दुःख में समान है, आसक्ति से रहित है।
verses.english_meaning: Equal to enemy and friend, same in honor and dishonor, equal in cold and heat, pleasure and pain, free from all attachment.
verses.context: The pairs of opposites as the testing ground of sama-darshana: enemy/friend, honour/dishonour, pleasure/pain, heat/cold.
verses.practical_application: Your triggers are a map of where you lack samatvam. If praise inflates you and criticism deflates you, that is where the work is. These pairs name exactly where ego still operates — and where sadhana should be applied.

verses.sanskrit:
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी सन्तुष्टो येन केनचित् |
अनिकेतः स्थिरमतिर्भक्तिमान्मे प्रियो नरः ||
verses.transliteration: tulya-nindastutir mauni santushtho yena kenachit | aniketah sthira-matir bhaktiman me priyo narah
verses.hindi_meaning: जिसे निन्दा और स्तुति समान है, जो मौनी है, जो जो कुछ मिले उसमें सन्तुष्ट है, जिसका कोई निश्चित घर नहीं, जो स्थिर-मति और भक्तिमान है — वह मनुष्य मुझे प्रिय है।
verses.english_meaning: One to whom praise and blame are equal, who is silent, content with whatever comes, who has no fixed home, who is steady-minded and devoted — such a person is dear to Me.
verses.context: The final 'priya' verse of the dedicated devotee portrait — mauna (silence), contentment with whatever comes, inner homelessness.
verses.practical_application: Mauni (one given to silence) is not just about speaking less — it is inner silence where the self-justifying mental commentary quiets. This inner silence is what daily meditation progressively builds.

verses.sanskrit:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतमिदं यथोक्तं पर्युपासते |
श्रद्दधाना मत्परमा भक्तास्तेऽतीव मे प्रियाः ||
verses.transliteration: ye tu dharmyam ritam idam yathauktam paryupasate | shraddadhana mat-parama bhaktas te tiva me priyah
verses.hindi_meaning: जो श्रद्धावान् लोग इस धर्ममय अमृत का यथोक्त रूप से सेवन करते हैं और मुझे परम मानते हैं — वे भक्त मुझे अत्यन्त प्रिय हैं।
verses.english_meaning: Those who, with faith and with Me as the Supreme, follow this immortal path of righteousness as taught — those devotees are exceedingly dear to Me.
verses.context: The closing verse of Chapter 12: following the dharma taught by the Gita with shraddha (faith) is the surest path to becoming dear to God.
verses.practical_application: Shraddha (faith) is the key ingredient that transforms mechanical practice into living sadhana. Practice with trust — trust in the teaching, trust in the process, trust that transformation is happening even when you cannot see it.

verses.sanskrit:
अमानित्वमदम्भित्वमहिंसा क्षान्तिरार्जवम् |
आचार्योपासनं शौचं स्थैर्यमात्मविनिग्रहः ||
verses.transliteration: amaanitvam adambhitvam ahimsa ksantir arjavam | acharyopasanam shaucham sthairyam atma-vinigrahah
verses.hindi_meaning: अमानित्व (नम्रता), दम्भहीनता, अहिंसा, क्षमा, सरलता, आचार्य की उपासना, शौच (शुद्धता), स्थिरता और आत्मनिग्रह।
verses.english_meaning: Humility, absence of pride, non-violence, forbearance, uprightness, service to the teacher, purity, steadiness, and self-control.
verses.context: The first of two verses listing the 'knowledge' qualities (jnana-lakshanas) — the character of the genuine seeker.
verses.practical_application: Amanitvam (humility) and adambhitvam (absence of show) are the first two — the antidotes to the performance-oriented ego that undermines authentic sadhana. Practice in private; don't advertise your streak.

verses.sanskrit:
इन्द्रियार्थेषु वैराग्यमनहङ्कार एव च |
जन्ममृत्युजराव्याधिदुःखदोषानुदर्शनम् ||
verses.transliteration: indriyartheshu vairagyam anahankara eva cha | janma-mrityu-jara-vyadhi-duhkha-doshanu-darshanam
verses.hindi_meaning: इन्द्रिय-विषयों में वैराग्य, अहंकार का अभाव और जन्म, मृत्यु, वृद्धावस्था, व्याधि और दुःखों के दोषों का बार-बार दर्शन करना।
verses.english_meaning: Dispassion toward sense objects, absence of ego, and constant perception of the evils in birth, death, old age, disease, and sorrow.
verses.context: The contemplative practices that build vairagya: consistently seeing the inherent flaws in the sensory world and the suffering cycle.
verses.practical_application: Janma-mrityu-jara-vyadhi-duhkha-dosha-anudarshanam means repeatedly contemplating the suffering inherent in the body and world. This is maranam-anusmriti-type practice — seeing clearly cultivates natural vairagya without forced suppression.

verses.sanskrit:
असक्तिरनभिष्वङ्गः पुत्रदारगृहादिषु |
नित्यं च समचित्तत्वमिष्टानिष्टोपपत्तिषु ||
verses.transliteration: asaktir anabhishvangah putra-dara-grihadishu | nityam cha sama-chittatvam ishtanishtopapatttishu
verses.hindi_meaning: पुत्र, स्त्री, घर आदि में अनासक्ति और अनभिष्वङ्ग, और इष्ट-अनिष्ट प्राप्त होने पर सदा समचित्तता।
verses.english_meaning: Non-attachment and non-identification with son, wife, home, and so on, and a constant evenness of mind toward all desired and undesired events.
